era_context: The early 13th century sees the Fifth Crusade in its final phase in Egypt, the Ayyubid sultanate fragmented into regional powers, the Mongol confederation consolidating under Temüjin (soon to be proclaimed Genghis Khan), the Jin Dynasty controlling northern China, and the Song Dynasty in the south. In Europe, the Angevin and Capetian dynasties remain in conflict; England is under the rule of King Henry III (from 1216). Islamic civilization is experiencing cultural and scientific flourishing despite political fragmentation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (no written historical records)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in most regions)

events.title: Fifth Crusade: Capture of Damietta (or Final Retreat)
events.description: The Fifth Crusade either captures Damietta in this year (according to some sources) or abandons the city and retreats (according to others). The chronology of the Fifth Crusade's final phase is disputed among historians. Some sources place the capture of Damietta in 1221, others in 1224, and the retreat in 1223. The siege, which began in 1219, was one of the longest and most costly of the Crusades. The Crusaders' inability to capitalize on their capture of Damietta and their subsequent retreat marked a major failure of the Fifth Crusade and a significant setback for the Crusader movement.
events.certainty_note: The dating of the Fifth Crusade's final phase is disputed. Some sources place the capture of Damietta in 1221, others in 1224. The retreat is sometimes dated to 1221, sometimes to 1223. The chronological uncertainty reflects differences in how sources mark the siege, capture, and retreat phases.

events.title: Death of Muhammad of Ghor; Fragmentation of Ghorid Empire
events.description: Muhammad of Ghor (Muhammad Ghori), the founder of the Delhi Sultanate and conqueror of northern India, dies (or is assassinated by Assassins). His death marks the beginning of the rapid fragmentation of the Ghorid empire. His former slave-generals (Mamluks), particularly Qutb al-Din Aibak in Delhi and others in the Deccan, establish independent sultanates. This transition from Ghorid rule to Mamluk sultanates reshapes the political landscape of South Asia and Central Asia. The exact date of Muhammad of Ghor's death is disputed; some sources place it in 1206, others in 1207 or later.
key_figures: Qutb al-Din Aibak (Mamluk general, future Delhi Sultan)
events.certainty_note: Muhammad of Ghor's death is traditionally dated to 1206 CE in most Islamic sources, but some sources place it in 1207 or later. The date 1221 appears to be an error or confusion with another event. The most reliable sources place his death in 1206.
cross_references: 1206-002 (Delhi Sultanate founded by Qutb al-Din Aibak)

events.title: Mongol Consolidation: Temüjin Proclaimed Genghis Khan (or Consolidation Continues)
events.description: Temüjin continues his consolidation of the Mongol tribes and steppe peoples. Some sources place his formal proclamation as Genghis Khan in 1206, others in 1204, and some in the early 1220s. The exact date of his proclamation is disputed, but by 1221, Temüjin has clearly established himself as the supreme leader of the Mongol confederation. His consolidation of power creates the military and political infrastructure for the subsequent Mongol conquests of the 13th century. The unification of the steppe peoples under a single leader represents a major shift in Central Asian politics.
events.certainty_note: The exact date of Temüjin's proclamation as Genghis Khan is disputed. The Secret History and other sources give different dates (1204, 1206, or later). By 1221, Temüjin's consolidation of power was well-established, but the precise chronology of his proclamation is uncertain.

events.title: Ayyubid Consolidation: Al-Kamil Emerges as Dominant Ayyubid Ruler
events.description: Al-Kamil, the Ayyubid Sultan of Egypt, consolidates his power and emerges as the dominant figure within the fragmented Ayyubid sultanate. Following the failure of the Fifth Crusade (or its retreat in this year), Al-Kamil's successful defense of Egypt enhances his prestige and authority. He will play a key role in subsequent negotiations with the Crusaders, including the Sixth Crusade (1228–1229). Al-Kamil's consolidation of Egyptian power represents a shift in the balance of power within the Ayyubid sultanate and establishes Egypt as the center of Ayyubid power.
events.certainty_note: Al-Kamil's consolidation of power following the Fifth Crusade is well-documented, but the precise chronology varies across sources. The period 1221–1223 saw his emergence as the dominant Ayyubid ruler.

events.title: Jin Dynasty: Continued Decline and Mongol Pressure
events.description: The Jin Dynasty of northern China faces continued pressure from Mongol raids and incursions along its northern borders. The Jin Dynasty's military capacity is weakened by internal strife, economic problems, and resource constraints. The Jin government struggles to maintain control over its vast territories and to mount an effective defense against Mongol incursions. These problems in the early 1220s presage the larger Mongol invasion that will begin in 1205 (or 1211 by some reckonings) and ultimately lead to the Jin Dynasty's collapse in 1234.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)
Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jin Dynasty's decline and Mongol pressure in the early 1220s are attested in Chinese sources, but specific events for individual years are often uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Fifth Crusade's final phase is highly disputed. Some sources place the capture of Damietta in 1221, others in 1224. The retreat is sometimes dated to 1221, sometimes to 1223. This chronological uncertainty reflects differences in how sources mark the siege, capture, and retreat phases. Muhammad of Ghor's death is traditionally dated to 1206 CE, not 1221; the date 1221 appears to be an error. Temüjin's proclamation as Genghis Khan is traditionally dated to 1206 or 1204, not 1221, though the exact date is disputed.
historiographic_note: The year 1221 is moderately well-documented for the Islamic world, Crusader states, and East Asia, but with significant chronological uncertainties and gaps. Islamic chronicles were compiled decades or centuries after events and reflect court perspectives. Crusader sources are abundant but biased toward European viewpoints. Chinese sources on the Jin Dynasty are late compilations. Mongol history relies heavily on the Secret History, compiled in the 13th century, which contains legendary elements and uncertain chronology. The surviving record is heavily skewed toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, and much of Northern Europe have minimal documentation for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Fifth Crusade, which began in 1217, ended in failure and retreat in 1221 (or 1223, depending on the source).
Muhammad of Ghor's death (traditionally dated to 1206, not 1221) directly led to the fragmentation of the Ghorid empire and the rise of the Delhi Sultanate.
Temüjin's consolidation of power in the early 1220s was related to his proclamation as Genghis Khan, traditionally dated to 1206.
Al-Kamil's consolidation of power following the Fifth Crusade positioned him to negotiate with the Sixth Crusade in 1228–1229.
The Jin Dynasty's decline and Mongol pressure in the early 1220s contributed to the Jin Dynasty's eventual collapse in 1234.
